response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
As an older person, I believe that managing fake content on social media is a straightforward process. When I first started using these platforms at my age, it was challenging to differentiate between real posts and scams. However, after some practice, I learned simple ways to protect myself. First, always verify the source of any message or link before clicking on anything related to mechanics or cars—my profession might come up with tips and offers.

My father has a PhD in engineering, so he helped me understand how misinformation can be designed to look legitimate but is often too good to be true. I use my critical thinking skills from years of practical work as a mechanic; you learn not to trust things at face value because there's always room for error or deception.

Second, since I spend only one hour daily on social media (as per the profile), it forces me to stay focused and avoid getting overwhelmed by fake news. During that time, I prioritize official accounts from known brands rather than random ones. Also, my Christian faith gives me a sense of truthfulness—when something feels off, I pause for reflection.

A third point is reporting suspicious content; I do it when needed because maintaining good mental health requires protecting oneself from constant exposure to false information. Lastly, limiting time on social media helps filter out fake stuff naturally.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary:
Step-by-step derivations: First, verify the source and cross-reference with known reliable accounts (like my father's engineering knowledge). Second, use limited engagement time to reduce exposure (from my daily one-hour habit). Third, apply critical thinking from mechanical work experience—just as diagnosing engine problems requires checking symptoms carefully.

Fourth, rely on personal values like truthfulness in faith. Finally, report fake content when necessary for mental health protection.
